coachNote: You nailed the read: "the C stuff feels easy because I'm familiar with C — the harder keys will trip me up." Exactly right, so today we move there. The black keys (B♭/E♭/G♭) hands-together landed in the sweet spot yesterday, so B♭ and E♭ nudge up a notch and G♭ — your hardest — gets the most reps to clean it up. A♭ settles in for one more pass and we add D♭, the next flat key. Then the part you asked for: the chords and the pop loop leave the safety of C. Seventh voicings move into G, the arpeggio goes to G, and the four-chord loop gets harder two ways — hands-together in G, and a fresh run in F. Minor grows too: A harmonic minor goes hands-together, and D minor joins. Rate honestly and tell me which key bites hardest.

exercises.label: C major — hands together
exercises.cue: Quick wake-up, both hands. You own this — keep it loose and even, then move on.

exercises.label: B♭ major — hands together
exercises.cue: A 4 yesterday — push the tempo one notch. If the crossing slips, it's the thumb; fix that one spot.

exercises.label: E♭ major — hands together
exercises.cue: Also a 4 — faster now. Thumbs stay on the white keys, long fingers ride the black.

exercises.label: G♭ major — hands together
exercises.cue: Your hardest one (a 3) — so it gets the most reps. Hold the tempo, chase clean. Almost all black keys; let the shape settle under your hand.

exercises.label: B♭ major — left hand alone
exercises.cue: Still a 4 — keep earning it alone. Lead the 1→4 pivot cleanly. (E♭ LH graduated — it was easy yesterday.)

exercises.label: A♭ major — right hand
exercises.cue: Second pass at A♭ — a touch faster. Thumb on C and F, finger 3 starts you off.

exercises.label: D♭ major — right hand (new)
exercises.cue: Five flats, but a comfy shape — start on finger 2, thumb on F and C (the two white keys). Slow and clean.

exercises.label: A harmonic minor — hands together
exercises.cue: Easy hands-separate yesterday — now together, with the raised 7th. Hear the F→G♯ leap in both hands at once.

exercises.label: D natural minor — right hand (new)
exercises.cue: One flat (B♭). Same finger pattern as C major — a familiar shape in a new, darker key.

exercises.label: ii–V–I sevenths in C — Dm7 · G7 · Cmaj7
exercises.cue: The one chord shape that was a real 4 yesterday — keep it, a touch quicker. Let each four-note seventh ring.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 2 3 5 on each — the top note is the 7th

exercises.label: ii–V–I sevenths in G — Am7 · D7 · Gmaj7 (new key)
exercises.cue: Same ii–V–I shape you know in C, now in G (F is sharp). This is the move you asked for — out of the familiar key.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 2 3 5 on each — the top note is the 7th

exercises.label: G major arpeggio — right hand (new key)
exercises.cue: The C arpeggio was easy — same line in G now. Thumb passes under smoothly going up, over coming down.

exercises.label: I–V–vi–IV in G — hands together
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/1
K:G
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "G"[GBd] | "D"[DFA] | "Em"[EGB] | "C"[CEG] |]
[V:2] G,, | D,, | E,, | C,, |]
exercises.cue: Yesterday's G loop was easy one hand — now harder two ways: hands together, in G. Right hand triads, left hand names each root.
exercises.fingering: RH triads 1 3 5 · LH single root under each

exercises.label: I–V–vi–IV in F — new key
exercises.cue: The four-chord loop in F (one flat, B♭). Find the shapes by feel — F, C, Dm, B♭. A new key for the same familiar sound.
